news_summary: Advancements in technology and nature-based solutions are driving progress in carbon mitigation, renewable energy adoption, enhanced environmental monitoring, and the recovery of Antarctic whale populations.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées technologiques et les solutions fondées sur la nature favorisent les progrès en matière de réduction des émissions de carbone, d'adoption des énergies renouvelables, d'amélioration de la surveillance environnementale et de rétablissement des populations de baleines en Antarctique.
news_summary_es: Los avances tecnológicos y las soluciones basadas en la naturaleza están impulsando el progreso en la reducción de las emisiones de carbono, la adopción de energías renovables, la mejora de la vigilancia medioambiental y la recuperación de las poblaciones de ballenas antárticas.

articles.title: Why both trees and technology are important in the race to mitigate carbon emissions
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 28 April 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01300-6Different carbon‑removal approaches solve different problems, and pitting these technologies against each other could slow progress.
articles.content:
This year, two standards will be released to help govern corporate climate action. Both must address an issue that has become more divisive than the science warrants: what kinds of carbon removal should count towards corporations offsetting their carbon emissions. The first is the International Organization for Standardization’s first net-zero standard (ISO 14060), and the second is the final version of the Science Based Targets Initiative’s corporate rules on net zero.

Carbon credits are failing to help with climate change — here’s why

This debate has spurred on a contest between ‘nature based’ approaches, such as storing carbon in soils and planting forests, and engineered technologies, which capture carbon dioxide directly from the air and store it safely and permanently, for example underground.

This contest is intuitively appealing, but it is scientifically misleading and increasingly counterproductive. In my role advising companies on carbon removal, I have seen it muddy decisions at every level. This ranges from firms trying to plan credible routes to net zero — which aims to balance the amount of carbon emitted with the amount removed by 2050 — to governments and standards bodies trying to write the rules amid pressure from lobbyists on both sides of the debate.

Framing carbon removal as a choice between nature and technology obscures what actually matters: how various methods perform, when they can be scaled up and which specific climate problem they are meant to address. Timing is an important consideration for all of these issues.

Carbon offsets aren’t helping the planet — four ways to fix them

First, carbon removals could help to speed up global emissions reductions over the next two decades, when cumulative emissions will have the greatest influence on global warming. Removing carbon as soon as possible will deliver more climate benefits than will doing so later, even if the storage method is not permanent. This is where short-lived carbon removal — typically delivered through fast-scaling methods, such as combining trees and hedges on farms and extensive reforestation — can make a meaningful contribution.

Second, in the medium term, carbon removal will be needed to counterbalance the residual emissions from hard-to-abate industries, such as aviation, cement and steel, even once net zero has been achieved. At this stage, durability becomes crucial: some CO 2 persists in the atmosphere for thousands of years and must be neutralized with equally long-lived storage. For instance, CO 2 captured from the air can be stored for centuries in geological formations, turned into minerals on land or stored as dissolved carbon compounds in the ocean.

Third, removal methods will be needed to bring atmospheric concentrations of CO 2 down to safe levels over millennia. This Herculean task will need to utilize every carbon-removal method.

Net zero needs AI — five actions to realize its promise

These problems overlap, but they are not interchangeable. Bundling them into a single issue produces policies that are either inconsistent or operationally unrealistic. This confusion is now shaping real-world investment and technology-procurement decisions.
articles.reasoning: The article discusses the complementary roles of nature-based and technological carbon removal methods in mitigating climate change, highlighting the importance of integrated approaches and upcoming standards to govern corporate climate action. It addresses a significant global environmental challenge with detailed context and practical implications for policy and industry, offering hope for effective climate solutions.
articles.personality_title: Combining trees and technology to fight carbon emissions effectively
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Climate change is caused by too much carbon dioxide (CO2) in the air. To reduce this, companies and governments need ways to remove CO2. There are two main ways: nature-based methods like planting trees, and technology that captures CO2 directly from the air.

**What happened** – In 2026, two new rules will be introduced to guide how companies measure their efforts to reach 'net zero' carbon emissions. These rules come from important organizations: the International Organization for Standardization (ISO) and the Science Based Targets Initiative (SBTi). They aim to clear up arguments about which carbon removal methods should count toward balancing emissions.

**Impact** – The debate between using nature or technology alone is confusing and slows progress. Experts now say both are needed because they work in different ways and times. For example, planting trees can quickly remove carbon in the short term, while technology can store carbon safely for hundreds or thousands of years. Using both together helps tackle different parts of the carbon problem more effectively.

**What's next step** – With new rules coming, companies and governments will be able to plan better how to reduce carbon emissions. They can decide when to use fast methods like trees and when to invest in long-lasting technologies. This clearer guidance can lead to smarter actions and faster progress in fighting climate change.

**One-sentence takeaway** – New standards recognize that both nature and technology must work together to remove carbon and help companies reach net zero emissions by 2050.
articles.personality_title_fr: Associer arbres et technologie pour réduire efficacement les émissions de carbone
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le changement climatique est causé par trop de dioxyde de carbone (CO2) dans l’air. Pour réduire cela, les entreprises et les gouvernements ont besoin de méthodes pour enlever le CO2. Il y a deux façons principales : les méthodes naturelles comme planter des arbres, et la technologie qui capture le CO2 directement dans l’air.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En 2026, deux nouvelles règles seront mises en place pour guider la manière dont les entreprises mesurent leurs efforts pour atteindre la neutralité carbone. Ces règles viennent d’organisations importantes : l’Organisation internationale de normalisation (ISO) et l’Initiative Science Based Targets (SBTi). Elles visent à clarifier quels types de méthodes de capture de carbone doivent compter pour compenser les émissions.

**Impact** – Le débat entre utiliser uniquement la nature ou la technologie est source de confusion et ralentit les progrès. Les experts expliquent maintenant que les deux sont nécessaires car ils fonctionnent de façons et à des moments différents. Par exemple, planter des arbres peut rapidement retirer du carbone à court terme, tandis que la technologie peut le stocker en toute sécurité pendant des centaines ou milliers d’années. Utiliser les deux ensemble aide à résoudre plus efficacement différents aspects du problème du carbone.

**Prochaine étape** – Avec ces nouvelles règles, les entreprises et les gouvernements pourront mieux planifier comment réduire les émissions de carbone. Ils pourront décider quand utiliser des méthodes rapides comme les arbres et quand investir dans des technologies durables. Ces directives plus claires permettront des actions plus intelligentes et des progrès plus rapides contre le changement climatique.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les nouvelles normes reconnaissent que nature et technologie doivent travailler ensemble pour enlever le carbone et aider les entreprises à atteindre la neutralité carbone d’ici 2050.
articles.personality_title_es: Combinar árboles y tecnología para reducir eficazmente las emisiones de carbono
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El cambio climático es causado por tener demasiado dióxido de carbono (CO2) en el aire. Para reducirlo, las empresas y gobiernos necesitan formas de eliminar el CO2. Hay dos maneras principales: métodos naturales como plantar árboles, y tecnología que captura CO2 directamente del aire.

**Qué pasó** – En 2026, se introducirán dos nuevas normas para guiar cómo las empresas miden sus esfuerzos para alcanzar emisiones netas cero. Estas normas vienen de organizaciones importantes: la Organización Internacional de Normalización (ISO) y la Iniciativa de Objetivos Basados en la Ciencia (SBTi). Buscan aclarar qué métodos de eliminación de carbono deben contar para equilibrar las emisiones.

**Impacto** – El debate sobre usar solo naturaleza o tecnología es confuso y frena el progreso. Los expertos ahora dicen que se necesitan ambos porque funcionan de formas y tiempos diferentes. Por ejemplo, plantar árboles puede eliminar carbono rápidamente a corto plazo, mientras que la tecnología puede almacenarlo de forma segura durante cientos o miles de años. Usar ambos juntos ayuda a resolver diferentes partes del problema del carbono de manera más efectiva.

**Próximo paso** – Con las nuevas normas, las empresas y gobiernos podrán planear mejor cómo reducir las emisiones de carbono. Podrán decidir cuándo usar métodos rápidos como los árboles y cuándo invertir en tecnologías duraderas. Esta guía más clara puede llevar a acciones más inteligentes y un progreso más rápido para combatir el cambio climático.

**Resumen en una frase** – Las nuevas normas reconocen que la naturaleza y la tecnología deben trabajar juntas para eliminar carbono y ayudar a las empresas a alcanzar emisiones netas cero para 2050.
articles.image_prompt: A harmonious landscape where a flourishing forest with diverse trees and rich soil seamlessly transitions into a futuristic yet gentle depiction of carbon capture technology—represented by elegant, glowing geometric structures embedded underground beneath the earth—bathed in warm, natural hues of green, brown, and soft gold, symbolizing the complementary roles of nature-based and engineered carbon removal working together for a sustainable future.

articles.title: Nighttime Imaging Grows Landsat’s Science Value
articles.summary: By Earth Resources Observation and Science (EROS) Center  For more than 50 years, Landsat has imaged Earth’s land and near-shore surfaces as the satellites descend in midmorning orbit, when daily sunlight is optimal. That’s just what they’ve always done.  Currently, Landsat 8 and Landsat 9 circle the globe while also making better use of their ascending paths, […]
articles.content: By Earth Resources Observation and Science (EROS) Center For more than 50 years, Landsat has imaged Earth’s land and near-shore surfaces as the satellites descend in midmorning orbit, when daily sunlight is optimal. That’s just what they’ve always done. Currently, Landsat 8 and Landsat 9 circle the globe while also making better use of their ascending paths, peering into the darkness for special requests. The visible spectral bands of Landsat—the same blue, green and red wavelength colors our eyes can see—are typically not that useful when collected on the ascending orbit node (also known as “nighttime imagery”). The exception is twilight or darkness at Earth’s poles, which can provide a surprisingly clear observation in the thermal infrared spectral bands where snow, ice and water temperatures can be retrieved when the sun is at or below the horizon. Through the dark, shortwave infrared (SWIR) bands within Landsat’s Operational Land Imager (OLI) instrument can detect intense heat sources such as volcanoes or active fires, while the Thermal Infrared Sensor (TIRS) measures surface temperatures that range from geothermal geysers to solid ice. There is a growing interest in seeing what Landsat can capture as it ascends over the dark side of Earth, according to Dr. Christopher Crawford, the Landsat Project Scientist at the U.S. Geological Survey (USGS) Earth Resources Observation and Science (EROS) Center. Crawford leads and oversees Landsat’s long-term Earth data acquisition strategy for the USGS. “I’ve seen a noticeable uptick in the number of nighttime imaging special requests. That’s a very active and innovative measurement science area for Landsat right now,” Crawford said. “We have active volcanoes, we’ve got an ice environment that’s changing, and wildfire occurrences are increasingly growing into hazards that threaten human safety, infrastructure and wildlife, among other issues. Nighttime imaging is an all-purpose solution, kind of like Jiffy Baking Mix.” R. Greg Vaughan of the USGS Astrogeology Science Center does field work at Yellowstone National Park. USGS photo Sources/Usage: Public Domain Keeping an Eye on Volcanoes and Yellowstone A particular request for nighttime imagery that turned into a “systematic observation,” Crawford said, is Yellowstone National Park. The volcanic area’s 10,000 thermal features, such as geysers or hot springs or steam vents, can get hotter or colder, and they can appear or disappear. Crawford is fascinated by volcanoes in general and recognizes the value of imaging them day and night. After Landsat 9 launched in 2021, when two satellites with the same high-quality sensors would together yield an image of each area of land every eight days, it seemed like a good time to start a consistent annual campaign to capture active volcanoes at night, he said. To view this video please enable JavaScript, and consider upgrading to a web browser that supports HTML5 video Watch a video about imagery related to the discovery of a new thermal area in Yellowstone. Sources/Usage: Public Domain R. Greg Vaughan of the USGS Astrogeology Science Center, who researches active volcanoes, gave him a list. Vaughan has used Landsat data and other remote sensing methods to monitor changing thermal features in his role as the remote sensing lead for the Yellowstone Volcano Observatory. Vaughan also taught Crawford something about imaging Yellowstone’s thermal features at night—that the best season for locating them is during winter. That’s when the contrast between the heated features and the colder surrounding area is greatest. “The thing that I’ve probably taken away the most is that you have to acquire data to then understand what data to continue to acquire,” Crawford said. Vaughan spotted an exciting surprise when reviewing Landsat 8 nighttime TIRS data of Yellowstone acquired in April 2017. Comparing warm areas in the imagery to previously mapped thermal features, he found a “big blob of bright, warm pixels” that didn’t match anything on the map. After ruling out the possibility that it could be a thawing lake next to frozen land, he looked at the secluded area with daytime aerial imagery. The telltale signs of a new and growing thermal feature were there: bright hydrothermal-altered soil and dead and dying trees. A Landsat 8 nighttime thermal infrared image from April 2017 shows the Tern Lake area in Yellowstone National Park. In Yellowstone, temperatures are extremely cold at night in the winter, and most lakes are frozen (dark pixels). West Tern Lake seems to be thawing here; it might receive some thermal water inflow from nearby hot springs. The patch of bright (warm) pixels between West Tern Lake and the Tern Lake Thermal Area emerged over a period of roughly 20 years. Lakes are outlined in blue; known thermal areas are outlined in red; and the red triangles are individual thermal features that have been mapped. Image credit: R. Greg Vaughn, USGS Vaughan discussed his find and his use of Landsat data in a recent Eyes on Earth podcast episode produced by USGS EROS. “This is why I love Landsat 8 and 9 so much. These instruments acquire data regularly, not just during the day, but they can also be tasked to acquire data at night on a regular basis. And this is really critical for my work,” Vaughan said. Vaughan has been named a member of the current Landsat Science Team, a group of scientific and technical subject matter experts who provide analysis and advice to the Landsat Program. His research in that capacity will focus on active volcanoes. An aerial view of the area in the Landsat 8 nighttime image shows the new thermal area (center left) that R. Greg Vaughan spotted in the Landsat image. The existing Tern Lake Thermal Area is the bright white patch of ground in the upper middle part of the image. West Tern Lake is the dark area in the lower right, and Tern Lake is above that. Photo credit: Michael Poland, USGS Fires, Flares and Urban Areas Among Requests The fire community in the western United States also finds value in Landsat nighttime imagery, Crawford said—including the energy industry and its infrastructure. The Department of Energy’s Pacific Northwest National Laboratory submits annual special requests for proactive nighttime imaging of seasonal wildfires to support on-the-ground decision making. “We’ve done it three seasons in a row, and the results are pretty remarkable in terms of what we’re able to see,” especially with the SWIR bands, Crawford said. Those results compare well to airborne infrared sensing taken from low-altitude flights over the same wildfires. Landsat can also detect gas flares that are useful to oil and gas industry functions. “There are regular special requests submitted to monitor global sites that produce Liquefied Natural Gas, or LNG,” Crawford said. In addition, he sees requests for nighttime images over particular cities to map urban temperature, which may be higher than cooler surrounding areas. One recent request went beyond the already routine monitoring of active volcanoes in Iceland to encompass the entire country and coastline in a large seasonal campaign to survey overall volcanic activity. Crawford weighs this type of request carefully, posing these questions: “Does this advance the science mission? Is it serving the user community?” For Iceland, that was a yes. “I look for areas where Landsat imaging data may be underutilized, as well as areas for strategic science mission advancement and societal benefits, and in many ways, these growth areas can be enabled through the data acquisition process,” Crawford said. Landsat 8’s thermal infrared, shortwave infrared and near infrared spectral bands expose the Caldor Fire’s advancing edge south of Lake Tahoe in California in a nighttime image from August 29, 2021. A LEAP Forward A significant advancement in learning about Landsat’s nighttime capabilities came with the effort to monitor polar regions year-round, with leadership from former Landsat Science Team member Dr. Ted Scambos from the University of Colorado Boulder. The Landsat Extended Acquisition of the Poles (LEAP) campaign now routinely collects imagery over the polar regions, where few wintertime images had existed in Landsat’s data record before. The visible-to-shortwave infrared and thermal infrared spectral bands allow scientists to track changes in polar ice sheets, measure polar surface temperatures and examine the interaction of ocean water and ice shelves. The sun’s low angle is not much of a hindrance to imaging data quality, Crawford said in an Eyes on Earth episode about the LEAP campaign. “Snow and ice are still really bright mediums on the surface, and so even if the illumination is low, you can still see a lot of detail because of the high reflectivity.” Fortunately, nighttime imaging does not burden Landsat 8 and Landsat 9. “The instruments are always on, so it’s just a matter of whether we’re recording the data,” Crawford said. This twilight thermal infrared image of Petermann Glacier, Greenland, was captured by Landsat 9 on January 5, 2024. This winter image displays data acquired when the sun was below the horizon. Darker areas are relatively colder than bright areas. The imagery’s darkness helps keep data volumes much lower than the daytime and allows sufficient time for the satellites to pass off the data to ground stations around the globe whose function is to downlink the recorded data. “We’re starting to leverage Landsat 8 and Landsat 9 observatory capabilities to maximum scientific and societal benefit returns,” Crawford said. “We’re populating the Landsat archive with long-term image data records that are helpful for not only quantifying changes on the Earth’s surface right now, but in the past and in the future.” Requesting and Accessing Imagery To learn more about Landsat data acquisitions and to submit a special request for future nighttime imagery, visit the Landsat Acquisitions webpage. All imagery collected by special requests is made available to the public through the USGS EarthExplorer website. Select the “Landsat Collection 2 Level-1” dataset, and then select “Night” under Additional Criteria.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant advancement in Earth observation through Landsat satellites' enhanced nighttime imaging capabilities. This development has broad scientific and societal benefits, including monitoring active volcanoes, wildfires, urban heat, and polar ice changes, which are critical for environmental monitoring and disaster management. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a meaningful technological and environmental breakthrough with tangible positive impacts.
articles.personality_title: Landsat satellites expand Earth monitoring with new nighttime imaging
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context**
For over 50 years, Landsat satellites have taken pictures of Earth's land and nearby waters during daylight, using sunlight to capture clear images. These satellites orbit the Earth and have always focused on midmorning passes when the sun shines best.

**What happened**
Now, Landsat 8 and Landsat 9 are also using their nighttime passes to take special images in darkness. Using special sensors that detect heat and infrared light, the satellites can see things like active volcanoes, wildfires, and temperature changes in polar ice, even when the sun is not shining. Scientists have started regularly requesting these nighttime images, such as for Yellowstone National Park’s thermal features and to track wildfires and urban heat.

**Impact**
This nighttime imaging provides new ways to watch Earth's changing environment. For example, scientists discovered a new warm area near Yellowstone’s hot springs by comparing nighttime images over the years. Firefighters and energy companies use these images to monitor wildfires and gas flares safely and more effectively. The ability to see polar ice and water temperatures during long winter nights helps track climate changes in remote areas.

**What's next step**
The program plans to continue growing its nighttime imaging capabilities, with more special requests from scientists and agencies worldwide. This will build a long-term archive of images that can help understand Earth’s changes over time, day and night. People interested in this data can request new images through the USGS website.

**One-sentence takeaway**
Landsat satellites now use nighttime imaging to reveal new details about Earth’s volcanoes, fires, cities, and polar ice, improving how we monitor our planet around the clock.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les satellites Landsat étendent la surveillance de la Terre grâce à l’imagerie nocturne
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte**
Depuis plus de 50 ans, les satellites Landsat prennent des images des terres et des eaux proches de la Terre pendant la journée, en utilisant la lumière du soleil pour obtenir des images claires. Ces satellites tournent autour de la Terre et se concentraient toujours sur les passages en milieu de matinée, quand le soleil brille le mieux.

**Ce qui s’est passé**
Aujourd’hui, Landsat 8 et Landsat 9 utilisent aussi leurs passages nocturnes pour prendre des images spéciales dans l’obscurité. Grâce à des capteurs spéciaux qui détectent la chaleur et la lumière infrarouge, les satellites peuvent voir des choses comme des volcans actifs, des feux de forêt et des changements de température dans la glace polaire, même sans lumière du soleil. Les scientifiques demandent régulièrement ces images nocturnes, par exemple pour les sources chaudes du parc de Yellowstone ou pour suivre les incendies et la chaleur urbaine.

**Impact**
Cette imagerie nocturne offre de nouvelles façons d’observer les changements de l’environnement terrestre. Par exemple, les scientifiques ont découvert une nouvelle zone chaude près des sources chaudes de Yellowstone en comparant des images nocturnes sur plusieurs années. Les pompiers et les compagnies d’énergie utilisent ces images pour surveiller les incendies et les torchères de gaz plus efficacement et en toute sécurité. La capacité à voir la glace polaire et la température de l’eau pendant les longues nuits d’hiver aide à suivre les changements climatiques dans des zones éloignées.

**Prochaine étape**
Le programme prévoit de continuer à développer ses capacités d’imagerie nocturne, avec davantage de demandes spéciales venant de scientifiques et d’organismes du monde entier. Cela permettra de créer une archive d’images à long terme pour mieux comprendre les changements de la Terre au fil du temps, de jour comme de nuit. Les personnes intéressées peuvent demander de nouvelles images via le site web de l’USGS.

**En une phrase**
Les satellites Landsat utilisent désormais l’imagerie nocturne pour révéler de nouveaux détails sur les volcans, les incendies, les villes et la glace polaire, améliorant ainsi la surveillance de la planète 24 heures sur 24.
articles.personality_title_es: Los satélites Landsat amplían la vigilancia de la Tierra con imágenes nocturnas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto**
Durante más de 50 años, los satélites Landsat han tomado fotos de la tierra y las aguas cercanas durante el día, usando la luz del sol para obtener imágenes claras. Estos satélites orbitan la Tierra y siempre se han enfocado en las pasadas de media mañana, cuando el sol brilla mejor.

**Qué pasó**
Ahora, Landsat 8 y Landsat 9 también usan sus pasadas nocturnas para tomar imágenes especiales en la oscuridad. Usando sensores especiales que detectan calor y luz infrarroja, los satélites pueden ver volcanes activos, incendios forestales y cambios de temperatura en el hielo polar, incluso cuando no hay luz solar. Los científicos han comenzado a pedir estas imágenes nocturnas regularmente, por ejemplo para las características térmicas del Parque Yellowstone y para monitorear incendios y el calor en las ciudades.

**Impacto**
Esta imagen nocturna ofrece nuevas maneras de observar el cambio ambiental en la Tierra. Por ejemplo, los científicos descubrieron una nueva zona caliente cerca de las fuentes termales de Yellowstone comparando imágenes nocturnas a lo largo de los años. Los bomberos y las compañías de energía usan estas imágenes para monitorear incendios y quemas de gas de forma más segura y efectiva. La capacidad de ver el hielo polar y las temperaturas del agua durante las largas noches de invierno ayuda a seguir los cambios climáticos en zonas remotas.

**Próximo paso**
El programa planea seguir desarrollando sus capacidades de imagen nocturna, con más solicitudes especiales de científicos y agencias de todo el mundo. Esto creará un archivo de imágenes a largo plazo para entender mejor los cambios en la Tierra con el tiempo, de día y de noche. Las personas interesadas pueden solicitar nuevas imágenes en el sitio web del USGS.

**Resumen en una frase**
Los satélites Landsat ahora usan imágenes nocturnas para mostrar nuevos detalles sobre volcanes, incendios, ciudades y hielo polar, mejorando la vigilancia de la Tierra las 24 horas del día.
articles.image_prompt: A serene nighttime landscape painting depicting a glowing volcanic mountain and steaming geysers symbolizing Yellowstone’s thermal activity, with a softly illuminated polar ice sheet in the background showing subtle thermal contrasts, all under a deep twilight sky, rendered in warm, earthy tones and gentle blues to evoke scientific discovery and natural wonder.

articles.title: How I pay almost nothing to power my house and electric car
articles.summary: The ongoing closure of the Strait of Hormuz has seen energy prices soar, but Alice Klein pays just A$25 (£13) a month for her electricity, even when charging an electric car or running an air conditioner.
articles.content:
The ongoing closure of the Strait of Hormuz has seen energy prices soar, but Alice Klein pays just A$25 (£13) a month for her electricity, even when charging an electric car or running an air conditioner.

Solar panels offer the possibility of a home with super-low energy bills Mujie/Alamy

Australia is in the midst of a home battery-installation frenzy, as households scramble to take advantage of a subsidy introduced by the federal government. My household is one of 300,000 that have already installed one of these subsidised batteries, and, much to my delight, I am now running my house and electric vehicle almost for free.

I have been obsessed with home batteries since I first wrote about them for New Scientist in 2018. They store energy captured from solar panels on your roof, so you can use it to power your home and, if you have one, an electric vehicle. By generating and storing all your own energy on your premises, you can reduce your carbon footprint and decouple yourself from price shocks in the external energy market, as we are currently experiencing amid the ongoing tussle over the Strait of Hormuz.

For a long time, the hefty price tags on home batteries made them a pipe dream for myself and most other people. But in recent years, their costs have been falling, even without government subsidies, putting them more in reach.

In my case, I first had to move out of pricey Sydney to meet the initial requirement of owning a home. My husband, children and I moved to a regional town about 400 kilometres north, where we could afford the mortgage on a plain but solid 1970s brick house. We installed 13 solar panels (6.6 kilowatt overall – the most common size in Australia) on our roof to take advantage of the ample sunshine and purchased a relatively cheap Chinese electric car.

The solar panels allowed us to run our dishwasher, washing machine, fridge, electric hot-water system, induction cooktop and other appliances for free on sunny days. But after the sun went down, and on cloudy and rainy days, we had to switch back to using grid electricity. If we charged our car overnight or ran our air conditioning on sweltering summer evenings, we paid for it.

We looked into getting an average-sized 15 kilowatt-hour home battery to store energy from our solar panels for nighttime use, but the cost – A$20,000 (£10,000) – scared us off. When the government introduced its subsidy scheme in July 2025, it brought the price down to A$13,000 (£6,500). We decided this was doable, especially because we could opt to pay it off interest-free over five years.

The battery system we installed contains three lithium iron phosphate (LiFePO₄) battery packs encased in a sleek white box, which is attached to a shaded wall on the outside of our house, to avoid the risk of overheating.

Our bills for electricity usage have since plummeted to around A$25 (£13) per month, despite us cranking our air conditioning overnight. This is a small fraction of the average A$300 (£150) we used to pay before we had solar panels and a battery, although we are also still required to pay a small fixed supply charge to our electricity provider that covers things like meter reading and maintenance of the grid’s poles and wires.

To try to get our costs down further, we have just signed up to a virtual power plant. This connects thousands of households with home batteries so that any surplus energy in individual batteries can be shared. We will be paid up to A$300 (£150) per year for sharing our surplus battery energy, which could cancel out our electricity-usage costs altogether.

Happily, our battery shielded us from the recent spike in fuel prices that occurred after the blockage of the Strait of Hormuz. Petrol prices in Australia went up by 40 per cent and diesel by 70 per cent, but we have continued to run our car for next to nothing. It also kept our lights on during an hour-long blackout on our street one night last week.

Of course, not everyone can afford a home battery, or indeed a home, but there are ways of making this technology more accessible. For example, in the state of South Australia, residents of government-owned social housing can apply to have solar panels and batteries installed on their properties for free to lower their energy bills.

Some people are worried about home batteries catching on fire, but the risk appears to be low. A recent study in Germany found that home battery fires are 50 times less likely than general house fires, four times less likely than electric vehicle battery fires and about on par with tumble dryer fires. Some types of home batteries, including lithium iron phosphate ones, are considered safer than others.

The Australian government’s home-battery scheme has been so wildly popular that it has just had to top it up with another A$5 billion (£2.65 billion), although it will also reduce individual subsidies next month to make the budget stretch to more households. The aim is to have 2 million home batteries installed in Australia by 2030.

Elsewhere, California has also introduced a government subsidy for home batteries and over 200,000 have been installed there to date.

The UK has been slower to adopt the technology, with around 20,000 home batteries currently being installed each year, but the UK government recently announced a plan to introduce a subsidy to encourage greater uptake.

Some of the technologies I’ve written about over the years have admittedly been a bit silly, like robots that give massages or play golf and tiny cars that rats can drive, but home batteries seem like the real deal. It’s satisfying to see them finally taking off and to be able to take advantage of one myself. With all the money we save, maybe we’ll even be able to afford that robot massage therapist.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a real-world example of a household significantly reducing energy costs and carbon footprint by using solar panels and home batteries, supported by government subsidies. The story highlights broad societal benefits such as energy independence, reduced emissions, and resilience to energy price shocks, with enough detail on technology, policy, and impact. It also mentions large-scale adoption goals and similar initiatives in other regions, indicating significant scope and positive environmental impact.
articles.personality_title: How solar panels and home batteries cut one family's electricity costs to almost nothing
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Energy prices are rising worldwide, especially after the Strait of Hormuz was closed, making fuel more expensive. Many people struggle to pay high electricity bills for their homes and electric cars.

**What happened** – Alice Klein and her family in Australia installed solar panels and a government-subsidized home battery system on their house. This lets them store energy from the sun and use it anytime, even at night. Because of this, their electricity bill dropped to about A$25 (£13) a month, much less than the average bill before.

**Impact** – Their system not only saves money but also protects them from sudden price rises in energy. For example, when fuel prices jumped by 40–70%, their electric car still cost almost nothing to charge. The battery also kept their lights on during a blackout. The Australian government’s program has helped 300,000 homes install batteries and plans to reach 2 million by 2030, showing a big shift toward clean, affordable energy.

**What's next step** – Alice’s family joined a virtual power plant, which links many home batteries together to share extra energy. They can earn money by sharing power with others, possibly making their electricity free. Other places like California and the UK are starting similar subsidy programs to encourage more people to use home batteries.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Using solar panels and home batteries, supported by government help, can greatly lower energy bills and protect families from rising fuel prices.
articles.personality_title_fr: Comment les panneaux solaires et les batteries domestiques ont presque éliminé la facture d’électricité d’une famille
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les prix de l’énergie augmentent dans le monde entier, surtout après la fermeture du détroit d’Hormuz, ce qui rend le carburant plus cher. Beaucoup de gens ont du mal à payer leurs factures d’électricité élevées pour leur maison et leur voiture électrique.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Alice Klein et sa famille en Australie ont installé des panneaux solaires et un système de batterie domestique subventionné par le gouvernement. Cela leur permet de stocker l’énergie du soleil pour l’utiliser à tout moment, même la nuit. Grâce à cela, leur facture d’électricité est tombée à environ 25 A$ (13 £) par mois, bien moins que la moyenne avant.

**Impact** – Leur système permet non seulement d’économiser de l’argent, mais aussi de se protéger contre les hausses soudaines des prix de l’énergie. Par exemple, lorsque les prix du carburant ont augmenté de 40 à 70 %, leur voiture électrique a continué à coûter presque rien à recharger. La batterie a aussi maintenu l’électricité pendant une panne de courant. Le programme gouvernemental australien a aidé 300 000 foyers à installer des batteries et vise 2 millions d’ici 2030, montrant un grand tournant vers une énergie propre et abordable.

**Prochaine étape** – La famille d’Alice a rejoint une centrale électrique virtuelle qui relie de nombreuses batteries domestiques pour partager l’énergie excédentaire. Ils peuvent gagner de l’argent en partageant leur énergie, ce qui pourrait rendre leur électricité gratuite. D’autres régions comme la Californie et le Royaume-Uni lancent des programmes similaires pour encourager l’usage des batteries domestiques.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Utiliser des panneaux solaires et des batteries domestiques, avec l’aide du gouvernement, peut fortement réduire les factures d’énergie et protéger les familles contre la hausse des prix du carburant.
articles.personality_title_es: Cómo los paneles solares y las baterías domésticas reducen casi a cero la factura eléctrica de una familia
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los precios de la energía están subiendo en todo el mundo, especialmente tras el cierre del estrecho de Ormuz, que ha encarecido el combustible. Muchas personas tienen dificultades para pagar sus altas facturas de electricidad para sus casas y coches eléctricos.

**Qué pasó** – Alice Klein y su familia en Australia instalaron paneles solares y un sistema de baterías domésticas subvencionado por el gobierno. Esto les permite almacenar la energía del sol y usarla en cualquier momento, incluso de noche. Gracias a esto, su factura de electricidad bajó a unos A$25 (£13) al mes, mucho menos que la media antes.

**Impacto** – Su sistema no solo ahorra dinero, sino que también los protege de subidas repentinas en los precios de la energía. Por ejemplo, cuando los precios del combustible subieron entre un 40 y un 70 %, su coche eléctrico siguió costando casi nada para cargar. La batería también mantuvo la luz durante un apagón. El programa del gobierno australiano ha ayudado a 300,000 hogares a instalar baterías y planea llegar a 2 millones para 2030, mostrando un cambio importante hacia energía limpia y asequible.

**Próximo paso** – La familia de Alice se unió a una planta de energía virtual que conecta muchas baterías domésticas para compartir el exceso de energía. Pueden ganar dinero compartiendo energía, lo que podría hacer que su electricidad sea gratis. Otros lugares como California y el Reino Unido están comenzando programas similares para fomentar el uso de baterías domésticas.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Usar paneles solares y baterías domésticas, con ayuda del gobierno, puede reducir mucho las facturas de energía y proteger a las familias de la subida de precios del combustible.
articles.image_prompt: A cozy 1970s brick house bathed in soft sunlight, its rooftop covered with neat solar panels, beside a sleek white home battery box mounted on a shaded exterior wall, while a simple electric car rests nearby; warm earthy tones and gentle brushstrokes convey energy independence and sustainable living in a peaceful regional setting.

articles.title: Antarctic whale populations are rebounding, but there’s still more to do
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM Good news: the Southern Ocean is filling with whales again. Humpback populations in Antarctica have nearly returned to pre-whaling levels, a rebound scientists say has been faster than almost anyone expected. Researchers conducting a survey near the South Orkney Islands this February recorded multiple groups of more than 100 […]
The post Antarctic whale populations are rebounding, but there’s still more to do first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM

Good news: the Southern Ocean is filling with whales again.

Humpback populations in Antarctica have nearly returned to pre-whaling levels, a rebound scientists say has been faster than almost anyone expected. Researchers conducting a survey near the South Orkney Islands this February recorded multiple groups of more than 100 feeding whales in a single day, scenes they described as “remarkable and breathtaking” and comparable to accounts written by the first polar explorers more than a century ago.

Since commercial whaling was banned in 1986, ending a period in which more than 2 million whales were killed in the Southern Ocean, the recovery has been steady. Blue whales, the world’s largest animals, have bounced back more slowly. That trend has held for nearly four decades.

“It is incredible that every day at the South Orkneys with decent weather, we could more or less guarantee seeing a group of 100-plus whales,” said Dr. Matt Savoca of Stanford University’s Hopkins Marine Station, who was aboard Sea Shepherd’s research vessel the Allankay for the survey. “The fact that groups this size are common here is what’s most remarkable. It’s breathtaking to see blows stretch from horizon to horizon, just as the first explorers to the region described over a century ago.”

A looming threat

Unfortunately, the same waters that once drew whalers now attract a different industry, and researchers say the timing could not be worse.

Industrial krill trawlers, some weighing up to 3,000 metric tonnes (roughly 3,300 US tons), operate in the same feeding grounds where the whales feed. The vessels fish for Antarctic krill, the small crustaceans that form the base of the Southern Ocean food web and the primary diet of whales, penguins, and seals.

What makes this particularly damaging, according to whale researcher Ted Cheeseman, is not just the volume of krill being removed but what happens to it. When whales consume krill, they return nutrients to the water through their waste, feeding the phytoplankton that krill populations depend on. Industrial trawlers, which process krill into dietary supplements, pet food, and aquaculture feed, pull those nutrients out of the ocean entirely.

“When a whale eats krill, it poops out krill,” said Cheeseman, who co-founded the citizen science organization Happy Whale. “There’s a nutrient recycling happening. If you take out more predators, you would imagine you get more prey. But when you take out whales, the krill reduces.”

The trawlers can be up to 100 times the size of a humpback whale. Cheeseman’s read: they have become a new kind of predator in the ecosystem.

Conservation through collaboration

The body responsible for protecting Antarctic marine resources, the Commission for the Conservation of Antarctic Marine Living Resources (CCAMLR), has been unable to act so far. In both 2024 and 2025, the commission failed to reach consensus on conservation measures after China and Russia blocked renewal of a key agreement restricting krill fishing in ecologically sensitive areas. Climate change is adding pressure of its own, with warming waters already affecting krill populations independently of fishing.

With no international agreement in sight, Savoca and Cheeseman are proposing a practical alternative: a voluntary 30-kilometer (19-mile) buffer zone around the South Orkney Islands where krill fishing would be banned. Krill fishers have already accepted similar buffers around penguin colonies, which gives the researchers some reason to think the industry might be open to the idea.

“There is an incredible opportunity for conservation through collaboration,” said Savoca. “The NGOs, scientists, and the fishing industry can come together and succeed where CCAMLR has failed.”

Whether that happens may determine whether this whale recovery continues or runs into the ceiling of what krill-depleted waters can actually support. The trawlers and the whale aggregations are operating in the same stretch of ocean right now. A buffer zone would not fix the diplomatic deadlock, but it could buy time while the politics catch up.

Did this solution stand out? Share it with a friend or support our mission by becoming an Emissary.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant positive environmental development: Antarctic humpback whale populations have rebounded to near pre-whaling levels, demonstrating a successful recovery after decades of protection. This recovery has broad ecological significance for the Southern Ocean ecosystem and the general public's interest in conservation. The article also provides detailed context about ongoing threats from industrial krill fishing and proposes a practical conservation solution, showing substance and potential for meaningful impact.
articles.personality_title: Antarctic humpback whales return in large numbers, but krill fishing remains a threat
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For many years, whalers hunted whales in the Southern Ocean near Antarctica, greatly reducing their numbers. Since commercial whaling was banned in 1986, scientists have watched whale populations slowly recover.

**What happened** – In early 2024, researchers near the South Orkney Islands saw groups of more than 100 humpback whales feeding together, a sight rarely seen since before whaling. This shows that humpback whales have nearly returned to the numbers they had before whaling began.

**Impact** – This recovery is important because it shows how a species can bounce back after being hunted almost to extinction. However, large fishing ships are now catching Antarctic krill, the small creatures whales eat. These ships remove krill and the nutrients whales help recycle, which could slow down whale recovery and harm the ocean ecosystem.

**What's next step** – Scientists and conservationists suggest creating a 30-kilometer (19-mile) no-fishing zone around the South Orkney Islands to protect krill and whales. This idea has support from some krill fishers and could help whales keep recovering while countries work on better rules.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Antarctic humpback whales are returning in large numbers after decades, but protecting their krill food is essential to ensure their continued recovery.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les baleines à bosse de l'Antarctique reviennent en grand nombre, mais la pêche au krill reste une menace
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Pendant de nombreuses années, les chasseurs de baleines ont réduit fortement le nombre de baleines dans l'océan Austral près de l'Antarctique. Depuis l'interdiction de la chasse commerciale en 1986, les scientifiques observent une lente reprise des populations de baleines.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Au début de 2024, des chercheurs près des îles Orcades du Sud ont vu des groupes de plus de 100 baleines à bosse se nourrir ensemble, un spectacle rare depuis avant la chasse à la baleine. Cela montre que les baleines à bosse ont presque retrouvé leur nombre d'avant la chasse.

**Impact** – Cette reprise est importante car elle montre qu'une espèce peut se remettre après avoir été chassée presque jusqu'à l'extinction. Cependant, de grands bateaux de pêche attrapent maintenant du krill antarctique, la nourriture des baleines. Ces bateaux enlèvent du krill et les nutriments que les baleines aident à recycler, ce qui pourrait ralentir la reprise des baleines et nuire à l'écosystème marin.

**Prochaine étape** – Des scientifiques et des défenseurs de la nature proposent de créer une zone de protection de 30 kilomètres autour des îles Orcades du Sud où la pêche au krill serait interdite. Cette idée est soutenue par certains pêcheurs de krill et pourrait aider les baleines à continuer de se rétablir pendant que les pays cherchent de meilleures règles.

**En une phrase** – Les baleines à bosse de l'Antarctique reviennent en grand nombre après des décennies, mais protéger leur nourriture, le krill, est essentiel pour assurer leur reprise durable.
articles.personality_title_es: Las ballenas jorobadas antárticas regresan en grandes números, pero la pesca de kril sigue siendo una amenaza
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante muchos años, los cazadores de ballenas redujeron mucho la cantidad de ballenas en el Océano Austral cerca de la Antártida. Desde que se prohibió la caza comercial en 1986, los científicos han observado una lenta recuperación de las poblaciones de ballenas.

**Qué pasó** – A principios de 2024, investigadores cerca de las Islas Orcadas del Sur vieron grupos de más de 100 ballenas jorobadas alimentándose juntas, una escena rara desde antes de la caza. Esto muestra que las ballenas jorobadas casi han vuelto a sus números anteriores a la caza.

**Impacto** – Esta recuperación es importante porque muestra cómo una especie puede recuperarse después de casi extinguirse. Sin embargo, grandes barcos pesqueros están capturando kril antártico, el alimento de las ballenas. Estos barcos extraen kril y los nutrientes que las ballenas ayudan a reciclar, lo que podría frenar la recuperación y dañar el ecosistema marino.

**Próximo paso** – Científicos y conservacionistas proponen crear una zona de protección de 30 kilómetros alrededor de las Islas Orcadas del Sur donde se prohíba la pesca de kril. Esta idea cuenta con apoyo de algunos pescadores y podría ayudar a las ballenas a seguir recuperándose mientras los países trabajan en mejores reglas.

**Una frase clave** – Las ballenas jorobadas antárticas regresan en grandes números tras décadas, pero proteger su alimento, el kril, es esencial para asegurar su recuperación continua.
articles.image_prompt: A serene Antarctic seascape at dawn, featuring a vast pod of graceful humpback whales breaching and feeding near the South Orkney Islands, with gentle waves stretching to the horizon; nearby, a large, yet unobtrusive krill trawler silhouette looms at a respectful distance, symbolizing cautious coexistence, all rendered in soft, natural blues, whites, and muted grays with warm golden light highlighting the hopeful recovery of the whale population.
